allies.rationale_text: Cognitive Load Theory helps the router choose simpler structures so complexity is processed without overload.
allies.source_quote: By routing complex ideas through simple organizational structures, concept maps, or analogies, the RMR ensures that new information is broken into absorbable chunks.

allies.rationale_text: Metacognitive reflection gives the router the self-monitoring needed to test whether the current mode is actually working.
allies.source_quote: Metacognition includes the planning aspect ("What are my goals?") and the monitoring aspect ("Could I learn this idea in a different way?").

antagonists.rationale_text: Confirmation bias hijacks routing by pushing the problem-solving path toward defending a preferred conclusion.
antagonists.source_quote: This bypasses objective mode selection, forcing the problem-solving effort to defend a preconceived idea.

antagonists.rationale_text: Commitment and consistency bias blocks route correction even when new evidence indicates another reasoning mode is needed.
antagonists.source_quote: This desire to avoid the "rigors of thinking" or the appearance of being inconsistent suppresses the RMR’s function to seek truth over comfort.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with representativeness-bias when familiar labels force premature mode routing.
structured_tensions.source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with representativeness-bias when familiar labels force premature mode routing.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with anchoring when the first framing locks route selection despite contradictory evidence.
structured_tensions.source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with anchoring when first framing locks route selection despite contradictory evidence.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with confirmation-bias when the router is tuned to validate a preferred mode instead of the real problem structure.
structured_tensions.source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when routers are tuned to confirm preferred modes rather than genuinely discriminating problem structure.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with commitment-and-consistency-bias when discomfort with inconsistency suppresses route correction.
structured_tensions.source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with commitment-and-consistency-bias (commitment and consistency bias) when the discomfort of appearing inconsistent suppresses route correction even when new evidence indicates a different reasoning mode is needed.

curation_notes.summary: The router source is close to relation-ready because it names both supporting discipline and explicit structured conflicts.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor topology or multihop routing artifacts in a first-order relation pilot.
open_questions: The raw source also contains inside-view pressure, but that relation is not cleanly targetable to a current canonical id.

allies: The raw ally section also gestures toward System 2 and multidisciplinary thinking, which may deserve richer treatment in a later wave.
antagonists: The raw inside-view/self-bias conflict was left deferred because the current canonical id surface does not expose that relation cleanly.
